full_markdown:
## Introduction
Picture a narrow blue ribbon of sea under a hard desert sun. On one side, Iran’s coast watches from the north; on the other, Oman and the United Arab Emirates hold the southern shore.[4] Huge tankers move through this corridor like slow metal cities, carrying the fuel that keeps factories running, planes flying, homes heated, and prices moving across the world.[4] This is the Strait of Hormuz: only about 50 kilometers wide at its entrance and exit, about 33 kilometers wide at its narrowest point, yet powerful enough to shake global markets when war approaches its waters.[4]

## Chapter 1: The Narrow Gate Between War and Trade
The Strait of Hormuz connects the Gulf to the Arabian Sea, making it the seaborne exit route for much of the Middle East’s oil and liquefied natural gas.[4] For a student hearing the name for the first time, the easiest image is a doorway: if the Gulf is a room full of energy supplies, Hormuz is the door through which many of those supplies must pass. About 20% of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas usually moves through this strait, which means a local military crisis can quickly become a global economic problem.[4]

The geography is simple, but its consequences are enormous. The northern shore belongs to Iran, while the southern side is shaped by Oman and the United Arab Emirates.[4] The passage is deep enough for the world’s largest crude oil tankers, so it is not just a regional waterway for small ships; it is one of the great highways of the modern energy system.[4] A single tanker may look lonely on the horizon, but its cargo is tied to petrol stations, electricity grids, airline costs, shipping bills, and household budgets far away.

The strait matters because it concentrates movement into a narrow space. A wide ocean gives ships room to scatter, reroute, and hide from danger. A chokepoint does the opposite. It squeezes commerce into predictable paths. In calm times, that makes trade efficient. In wartime, it makes ships vulnerable. Mines, missiles, drones, patrol boats, naval warnings, insurance costs, and fear can all turn a short stretch of water into a global alarm bell.

## Chapter 2: Why the Current Crisis Erupted
The recent crisis grew from the Iran war and the confrontation involving Iran, the United States, and Israel.[4] After the US and Israel attacked Iran on 28 February, Tehran effectively blocked the Strait of Hormuz, striking at one of the world’s busiest oil shipping channels.[4] That action transformed the strait from a map feature into the center of world attention, because ships were no longer simply crossing water; they were entering a zone where military power, national pride, and economic pressure collided.

Iran’s position gives it a dangerous kind of leverage. It does not need to control every ocean, every tanker, or every port. It only needs to threaten enough uncertainty in this narrow corridor to make shipowners hesitate, insurers raise prices, and governments panic. The logic is brutal: if Iran is attacked, Iran can make the world feel the consequences through energy prices. That does not mean closing Hormuz is easy or cost-free. It also hurts Iran’s neighbors, damages global confidence, and risks drawing outside navies deeper into conflict. But as a political signal, it is loud.

The United States views safe passage through such waterways as a core interest, especially when energy markets and allied economies are at stake. Iran views foreign military pressure near its coast as a threat to sovereignty and survival. Israel sees Iran through the lens of regional security and military danger. Each side acts from fear as much as ambition: fear of weakness, fear of attack, fear of losing control of the next move. That is why the Strait of Hormuz is not only about oil. It is about credibility. Whoever appears unable to protect ships, allies, or national territory risks looking weaker in the eyes of rivals.

## Chapter 3: The Ceasefire and the Promise of Safe Passage
The sharpest recent turning point came when Iran and the US agreed to a ceasefire on the condition that safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz would be guaranteed.[4] That phrase, “safe passage,” sounds calm, almost bureaucratic, but in practice it means tankers moving without harassment, military forces holding back, and governments trusting that the waterway will not become a battlefield again. A ceasefire at Hormuz is not just silence after gunfire. It is the reopening of a bloodstream.

Markets reacted quickly. Hostilities had sent global fuel prices soaring because about one-fifth of the world’s oil and LNG usually passes through the strait.[4] After the ceasefire announcement, oil prices fell by around 15%, showing how strongly traders connect the price of fuel to the security of this single corridor.[4] For an exam, this is one of the most important cause-and-effect chains to remember: conflict near Hormuz raises risk, higher risk pushes up energy prices, and a credible ceasefire can bring prices down.

The fall in prices also reveals something deeper about the modern world. Much of the global economy runs on expectations. If traders believe ships may be delayed, attacked, or forced to reroute, prices rise even before a shortage fully arrives. If they believe the route is reopening, prices can drop rapidly. The ships themselves move slowly, but fear moves instantly.

## Chapter 4: What Shipping Companies Fear
For shipping companies, the Strait of Hormuz crisis is not an abstract diplomatic story. It is a daily calculation of risk. A tanker captain approaching the strait must think about navigation, weather, crew safety, naval warnings, possible inspection demands, and the invisible pressure of politics. The corridor may be deep enough for giant crude oil tankers, but depth does not remove danger when missiles, mines, drones, or fast attack boats are part of the strategic picture.[4]

The shipping world is built on schedules and predictability. A tanker delayed by several days can disrupt refinery planning. A route judged unsafe can increase insurance premiums. A crew placed in danger can force companies to pause voyages. Even if only a few ships are directly threatened, the entire system reacts because no company wants to be the one that guessed wrong.

For countries that import energy, Hormuz is a reminder that energy security is not just about having money to buy oil or gas. It is about whether the route from producer to consumer remains open. For countries that export energy from the Gulf, the strait is both a gateway and a vulnerability. Their wealth may flow outward through the water, but that also means their economic future depends on the stability of a narrow maritime passage.

## Chapter 5: The Bigger Meaning for Global Order
The Strait of Hormuz is often described as a chokepoint, but it is also a test. It tests whether powerful states can prevent regional wars from damaging global trade. It tests whether military threats can override international shipping norms. It tests whether energy markets can remain stable when politics turns violent.

The crisis also shows the limits of distance. A student sitting in a classroom far from the Gulf might think the strait is someone else’s problem. Then fuel prices rise. Shipping costs shift. Governments issue warnings. News screens fill with maps of a place only 33 kilometers wide at its narrowest point.[4] Suddenly, a waterway between Iran, Oman, and the UAE becomes part of everyday life.[4]

For school exams, the key is to connect geography, economics, and politics. Geography explains why the strait is important: it is the narrow route between the Gulf and the Arabian Sea.[4] Economics explains why the world reacts: about 20% of global oil and LNG usually passes through it.[4] Politics explains why it becomes dangerous: Iran, the US, and Israel are locked in a confrontation where controlling risk at sea becomes a form of power.[4]

## Conclusion
The current events in the Strait of Hormuz are not just about ships on water. They are about a narrow place where war, trade, energy, and diplomacy meet. Iran’s effective blockade after the US and Israeli attacks on 28 February pushed fuel prices upward, while the ceasefire tied to guaranteed safe passage helped send oil prices down by about 15%.[4] The lesson is clear: in the modern world, a small strait can carry enormous weight. Hormuz is a reminder that geography still matters, and that the world economy can tremble when one narrow gate begins to close.
condensed_markdown:
## The Chokepoint
The Strait of Hormuz links the Gulf to the Arabian Sea, bordered by Iran, Oman, and the UAE.[4] At its narrowest, it is about 33 kilometers wide.[4]

### Key Insight
A tiny passage controls a huge share of global energy movement.

## Why It Matters
About 20% of the world’s oil and LNG usually passes through Hormuz.[4] When ships face danger there, fuel prices and shipping costs can jump worldwide.[4]

### Key Insight
Hormuz turns regional conflict into global economic pressure.

## The Current Crisis
After US and Israeli attacks on Iran on 28 February, Tehran effectively blocked the waterway.[4] Iran and the US later agreed to a ceasefire requiring guaranteed safe passage.[4]

### Key Insight
The ceasefire matters because it reopens the route.

## Market Shock
Hostilities sent fuel prices soaring, but oil fell about 15% after the ceasefire announcement.[4]

### Key Insight
Markets react fast when Hormuz opens or closes.
